attributes.stem: A 13-year-old girl presents to her General Practice clinic with her parent with a two-day history of fainting at school during biology class, where they have been studying animal biology and performing dissections. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits: pulse rate 72/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8°C.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Reassure and provide education on trigger avoidance
Refer to psychology for cognitive behavioural therapy
Advise increased salt and fluid intake
attributes.explanation:
This girl is experiencing symptoms of neurally mediated syncope. This occurs when there is rapid vasodilation and/or bradycardia which decreases cerebral perfusion, causing transient loss of consciousness. This condition can be further classified as vasovagal (commonly caused by emotional stress, as in this case), situational (during micturition, swallowing or sneezing) and carotid sinus syndrome. A typical history, non-specific physical examination and normal electrocardiogram is typically adequate to confirm the diagnosis but any features of an alternative syncopal cause should prompt further investigation. This condition is benign and is initially managed conservatively, with reassurance and education about trigger avoidance, adequate hydration and measures to avoid injury if symptoms were to reoccur.

A head-up tilt table test may be considered to confirm the diagnosis of neurally mediated syncope in patients with recurrent episodes. However, it is not routinely performed and would not be the initial management step in an otherwise well patient with a clear emotional trigger.

Cognitive behavioural therapy may be beneficial as an adjunct for some patients with recurrent vasovagal syncope triggered by anxiety. However, it would not be first-line and this patient has only had a single episode so far.

Increasing salt and fluid intake is sometimes advised as a lifestyle measure in patients with recurrent neurally mediated syncope, to increase intravascular volume. However, the initial management should focus on identifying and avoiding triggers.

24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring may be considered if orthostatic hypotension is suspected as the cause of syncope. However, the description of fainting during an emotional trigger in this case is more consistent with neurally mediated (vasovagal) syncope.
learningPoints: The initial management for neurally mediated syncope consists of reassurance and patient education.
references: Sheldon R, Connolly S, Rose S, Klingenheben T, Krahn A, Morillo C, Talajic M, Ku T, Fouad-Tarazi F, Ritchie D, Koshman ML; POST Investigators. Prevention of Syncope Trial (POST): a randomized, placebo-controlled study of metoprolol in the prevention of vasovagal syncope. Circulation. 2006 Mar 7;113(9):1164-70. doi: 10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.105.535161. Epub 2006 Feb 27. PMID: 16505178.

Sheldon R, Raj SR, Rose MS, Morillo CA, Krahn AD, Medina E, Talajic M, Kus T, Seifer CM, Lelonek M, Klingenheben T, Parkash R, Ritchie D, McRae M; POST 2 Investigators. Fludrocortisone for the Prevention of Vasovagal Syncope: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2016 Jul 5;68(1):1-9. doi: 10.1016/j.jacc.2016.04.030. PMID: 27364043.

Sheldon RSMDPFF, Grubb BPMDF, Olshansky BMDFFFC, Shen W-KMDFFF, Calkins HMDFC, Brignole MMDF, et al. 2015 Heart Rhythm Society Expert Consensus Statement on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Postural Tachycardia Syndrome, Inappropriate Sinus Tachycardia, and Vasovagal Syncope. Heart rhythm. 2015;12(6):e41-e63.
